summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a scalar quantity?

A scalar quantity has only magnitude (size) and no direction. Examples include mass, temperature, speed, energy, and time.

What is the resultant of two vectors of 3 N and 4 N acting at right angles to each other?

When two vectors are perpendicular, use the Pythagorean theorem: resultant = sqrt(3^2 + 4^2) = sqrt(9 + 16) = sqrt(25) = 5 N.

What is a vector quantity?

A vector quantity has both magnitude (size or amount) and direction. Examples include velocity, force, displacement, and acceleration.

What is the 'resultant' of two or more vectors?

The resultant is the single vector that produces the same effect as two or more vectors acting together. It is found by vector addition using the tip-to-tail or component method.

What is the difference between distance and displacement?

Distance is a scalar that measures the total path length traveled, regardless of direction. Displacement is a vector that measures the straight-line distance from start to finish with direction.

What is the definition of 'equilibrium' in terms of vectors?

An object is in equilibrium when the vector sum of all forces acting on it equals zero. This means the forces balance out and the object does not accelerate.

What is the term for the scalar version of velocity?

Speed is the scalar counterpart of velocity. Speed describes only how fast an object moves (magnitude), while velocity also includes the direction of motion.

A student walks 4 meters east and then 3 meters north. What is the magnitude of their displacement?

Since east and north are perpendicular, use the Pythagorean theorem: displacement = sqrt(4^2 + 3^2) = sqrt(16 + 9) = sqrt(25) = 5 meters. The distance traveled is 7 m, but displacement is only 5 m.

A student pushes a box with 10 N to the right while another pushes with 10 N to the left. What is the resultant force on the box?

When two equal forces act in opposite directions, they completely cancel out. The resultant is 10 N - 10 N = 0 N. The box is in equilibrium and will not accelerate.

A pilot flies 200 km north and then turns and flies 200 km south. What is the pilot's total displacement?

Displacement is the straight-line distance from start to finish. Since the pilot returned to the starting point, the displacement is zero, even though the total distance traveled was 400 km.

In a tug-of-war, Team A pulls with 500 N to the left and Team B pulls with 450 N to the right. What is the resultant force?

The forces act in opposite directions, so the resultant is 500 N - 450 N = 50 N in the direction of the larger force (to the left). Team A wins with a net force advantage of 50 N.

A hiker walks 6 km east, then 8 km north. What is the shortest distance back to the starting point?

The shortest distance back is the magnitude of the displacement: sqrt(6^2 + 8^2) = sqrt(36 + 64) = sqrt(100) = 10 km. The total distance walked (14 km) is greater than the displacement.

A boat crosses a river heading due north at 5 m/s while the current pushes it east at 5 m/s. What is the boat's actual speed relative to the ground?

Since north and east are perpendicular, the resultant speed = sqrt(5^2 + 5^2) = sqrt(50) = approximately 7.1 m/s. You cannot simply add the speeds because the velocities are at right angles.

A drone flies 30 m east, then 40 m north, and finally 30 m west. What is the magnitude of its total displacement?

The 30 m east and 30 m west cancel out, leaving only the 40 m north. The total displacement is 40 m north, even though the total distance traveled was 100 m.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: What does the term 'tip-to-tail method' refer to in vector addition?

In the tip-to-tail method, you draw the first vector, then place the tail (start) of the second vector at the tip (end) of the first. The resultant is drawn from the tail of the first vector to the tip of the last.
